name: Rotating Savings and Credit Associations (Roscas) — African Diaspora
location: African diaspora communities globally; documented extensively in North America, UK, Caribbean
anti_capture_mechanisms: community_membership_vetting: only known trusted members admitted — no external registration surface
no_state_certification: operates without legal incorporation, registration, or regulatory oversight
transaction_level_illegibility: individual transactions invisible to financial surveillance
trust_enforcement: social capital (not contracts) enforces obligations — no legal claim surface for capture
informal_structure: no named leadership, no formal charter, no asset base to seize or defund
oral_tradition: rules passed through practice and relationship, not documents
scale: Tens of millions of participants globally. Individual circles typically 5-50 members. Aggregate capital managed informally dwarfs formal credit union sector in some diaspora communities.

financial_dimension.mechanism: Members contribute fixed amounts at regular intervals; each cycle one member receives the full pot. Repeats until all members have received. No interest, no collateral, no credit check.
financial_dimension.anti_predatory: Excludes payday lenders, banks, and check-cashing services entirely — community controls capital flow
financial_dimension.startup_function: Historically used for business starts, immigration costs, housing deposits — capital formation outside the formal banking system
financial_dimension.trust_basis: Social consequence of default (exclusion, reputational damage) more effective than legal enforcement

source.primary: Hossein, Caroline Shenaz (2024). 'The Banker Ladies: How Black Women's Cooperatives Built Parallel Financial Systems.' University of Toronto Press. Open access via U of T Press.
source.note: Open access — freely citable. Hossein's framing: Roscas as parallel financial infrastructure built by and for communities excluded from formal banking. Not charity. Not informal. Deliberately structured resistance to financial exclusion.

why_this_fills_a_gap: Prior 5 cases (BPP, Zapatista, Minneapolis, Hillsboro, Paris Commune) covered governance, food distribution, service delivery, and operational security but had no financial infrastructure case. Roscas are the financial layer: how communities capitalized themselves outside state-legible systems for centuries before the cooperative movement had a theory for it.

connection_to_anti_capture_framework.bordiga_content: Active: participant community shows up at every cycle. Mutual obligation is the enforcement mechanism.
connection_to_anti_capture_framework.distributed_illegibility: No central fund to seize. No named institution to defund. Each circle is independent — destroying one doesn't touch others.
connection_to_anti_capture_framework.fourth_question: The deed IS the argument: the act of pooling capital outside the banking system demonstrates that communities can meet financial needs without state-approved intermediaries.
connection_to_anti_capture_framework.fifth_question: Historically connected to formal organizing: Roscas provided startup capital for cooperative businesses, funded immigration costs that expanded the community, sustained organizers. The financial infrastructure enabled organizational capacity.

notes: Hossein uses 'Banker Ladies' to name the women (predominantly) who organize and run Rosca circles — the conveners, not just participants. They function as informal financial institution presidents. The labor is gendered and largely uncounted in economic data.
